title: Exact metastability in a class of driven-dissipative quantum many-body systems
authors: David D. Noachtar, Aashish A. Clerk
abstract: Metastability in many-body quantum systems and its associated exponentially-long timescales have been the subject of considerable recent interest. Here, we focus on a class of driven-dissipative many-body open quantum systems described by a Lindbladian having hidden time-reversal symmetry (a form of quantum detailed balance). Examples include boundary-driven interacting spin chains, bosonic lattice models and driven-dissipative collective spin models. We suggest that for such systems, slow timescales in the vicinity of a dissipative first-order phase transition can be analytically predicted using a special purification of the non-equilibrium steady state. We show the accuracy of our conjecture through detailed studies of a dissipative transverse-field Ising model with collective and local decay, and a driven-dissipative nonlinear cavity model. Our results allow quantitative insights into metastability and slow dynamics for a range of systems, including cases where semiclassical or path-integral instanton approaches are intractable.
summary: This paper introduces an analytical method to determine slow relaxation rates (metastability) in open quantum many-body systems exhibiting hidden time-reversal symmetry. By purifying the non-equilibrium steady state, the authors derive an effective potential barrier whose height predicts the dissipative gap scaling ($\exp(-N\Delta V)$). This approach offers a powerful alternative to complex instanton calculations for understanding first-order phase transitions in driven quantum matter.
main_problem: The research addresses understanding metastability and exponentially-long timescales in open, driven-dissipative quantum many-body systems near first-order dissipative phase transitions (DPTs). The goal is to analytically predict the slow relaxation rate without relying on intractable path-integral methods.
main_result: The authors propose that for systems possessing hidden time-reversal symmetry (hTRS), the slow dynamics are governed by an effective potential barrier ($\Delta V$) derived directly from a special purification of the non-equilibrium steady state (NESS). This method successfully reproduces results from established instanton calculations.
method: The core methodology involves exploiting the existence of hTRS to construct a doubled system formalism. This allows for the analytical determination of the NESS purification, which in turn yields an effective potential $V(n)$ whose barrier height predicts the dissipative gap $\Gamma_{	ext{diss}} \sim e^{-N\Delta V}$.
model_or_system: The work analyzes general driven-dissipative open quantum systems described by Lindblad master equations. Specific models studied include boundary-driven spin chains, bosonic lattice models, and the Dissipative Transverse-field Ising Model (DTFIM). The analysis often employs a doubled-system formalism to enforce hTRS.
key_observables: Dissipative gap ($\Gamma_{	ext{diss}}$), Non-equilibrium potential barrier ($\Delta V$), Steady-state order parameter $\langle \hat{ho}_{	ext{ss}} angle$, and the purified steady state $|\Psi_T angle_{AB}$.
important_parameters: System size ($N$), control parameters (e.g., detuning, coupling strengths), and the scaling exponent $N$ in the exponential decay rate.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the existence of hidden time-reversal symmetry (hTRS) in the system dynamics. The analysis often relies on mean-field approximations or specific symmetries (like permutational symmetry for probability distributions).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how the derived potential $V_{	ext{hTRS}}(o)$ determines the gap, comparing it to naive potentials; they also show the ladder structure resulting from the doubled system mapping and compare calculated barriers against established instanton results.
paper_structure: The paper builds conceptually by first establishing the classical analogy for detailed balance, then generalizing this concept to quantum systems via hTRS using a doubled-system formalism. It applies this framework to specific models (like nonlinear cavities or DTFIM) to derive and compare the predicted potential barriers with known results.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, symmetry-based theoretical tool for calculating non-equilibrium dynamics in complex quantum systems where traditional methods fail. The ability to bypass path integrals by analyzing the NESS structure is highly valuable for AMO physicists studying driven cavity QED or superconducting circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Hidden time-reversal symmetry allows one to di- rectly predict the dissipative gap Γdiss (i.e. slowest relaxation rate) of open many-body Lindbladians (see inset). A special steady-state purification defines a potential function VhTRS(o) (solid, orange) whose barrier height ∆V controls the dissipa- tive gap; here o is an effective order parameter. This potential does not match a more naively-defined potential Vnaive(o) ob- tained directly from the steady-state density matrix (dashed, blue). Results are for a dissipative transverse-field Ising model (with o related to the magnetization), tuned near a first-order phase transition; see Appendix C 3 and Fig. 12).

FIG. 2. a) Generic plot of the steady-state average order parameter versus the relevant system parameter for a system exhibiting a first-order dissipative phase transition (DPT), for various system sizes N. At the DPT (dashed line) the steady state ˆρss abruptly changes from ˆρ−to ˆρ+. Slow dynamics are governed by the dissipative gap Γdiss, which close to the transition is akin to the slow switching rate between minima of a double-well potential. c) For systems having hTRS, despite not being in thermal equilibrium, we conjecture (and show for several examples) that ˆρss directly determines Γdiss. A special purification of ˆρss sets an effective potential barrier ∆V , which then sets Γdiss...

FIG. 3. Construction of the doubled system in the hid- den time-reversal symmetry framework. a) A quantum sys- tem with Hamiltonian ˆH is subject to Markovian dissipation shown here using two jump operators ˆL1 and ˆL2. b) This quantum system forms system A of the doubled system con- sidered in hTRS. System A and system B emit photons into chiral waveguides which only propagate photons from system A to system B, resulting in a cascaded quantum network [53]. Therefore system A is not impacted by system B while system B interacts with the radiation dissipated by system A. If the model has hidden time-reversal symmetry, the Hamiltonian and jump operators of system B are simply related to those...

IV A. a) The steady-state purification lives in a dark- state manifold D+ (top) which is mapped to an orthogonal manifold B−(bottom) under ˆHAB. However, these manifolds are unstructured which complicates solving Eq. (17). We ar- gue that this problem massively simplifies assuming Eqs. (21) and (26). b) Under these assumptions we can use a charge degree of freedom to arrange a special set of states in D+ (top) and B−(bottom) as two legs of a ladder which are cou- pled in a local fashion by ˆHAB (solid arrows). This simplified structure allows us to obtain analytical expressions for the purified steady state |ΨT ⟩AB using...

FIG. 5. Mapping the Hermitian ladder model to a tight- binding chain with inhomogeneous hopping amplitudes for the nonlinear cavity model in Sec. V A. Using the coupling coefficients hk,k and hk+1,k defined in Eq. (44) the effective Hamiltonian ˆH′ AB has the structure of a tight-binding chain with a two-site unit cell, corresponding to the dark states |dk⟩AB and bright states |bk⟩AB with charge k.
